section.title: David Anointed King and Jerusalem Taken
section.sectionRationale: With Saul's tragic end now behind the narrative, the Chronicler moves directly to David's coronation, skipping entirely the years in 2 Samuel when David reigned only over Judah while conflict continued with Saul's house. For the Chronicler, David's kingship begins the moment all Israel, with one voice, comes to anoint him according to the word of the LORD through Samuel, immediately followed by the capture of Jerusalem, the future site of the temple, and a roll call of the mighty men whose valor undergirded his reign.
section.thesis: All Israel gathers as one to anoint David king exactly as the LORD had spoken through Samuel, and David immediately takes Jerusalem, the city that will become the dwelling place of the ark and, later, the temple; together these events show a king established not by conquest or scheming but by the LORD's own word and the willing unity of His people, a pattern of gathered devotion around God's anointed that anticipates the greater gathering of the nations around Christ.
section.frameNote: Three main movements, arranged around a striking omission: all Israel anoints David king at Hebron; David and Joab capture Jerusalem, the future city of God's dwelling; and a lengthy roll call names the chiefs and company of David's mighty men, whose strength, given by the LORD, secured his kingdom.

units.label: All Israel anoints David king
units.note: according to the word of the LORD through Samuel

verses.text: Then all Israel came together to David at Hebron and said, "Here we are, your own flesh and blood.
Even in times past, while Saul was king, you were the one who led Israel out and brought them back. And the LORD your God said, 'You will shepherd My people Israel, and you will be ruler over them.'"

So all the elders of Israel came to the king at Hebron, where David made a covenant with them before the LORD. And they anointed him {{mashiach|king}} over Israel, according to the word of the LORD through Samuel.

units.label: David captures Jerusalem
units.note: the fortress of Zion becomes the City of David

verses.text: Then David and all the Israelites marched to Jerusalem (that is, Jebus), where the Jebusites inhabited the land.
The people of Jebus said to David, "You will never get in here." Nevertheless, David captured the fortress of {{tsiyon|Zion}} (that is, the City of David).
Now David had said, "Whoever is the first to strike down a Jebusite will become chief commander." And Joab son of Zeruiah went up first, and he became the chief.
So David took up residence in the fortress; that is why it was called the City of David.
He built up the city around it, from the supporting terraces to the surrounding wall, while Joab restored the rest of the city.
And David became greater and greater, for the LORD of Hosts was with him.

units.label: The chiefs of David's mighty men

verses.text: Now these were the chiefs of David's {{gibbor|mighty men}}, who, together with all Israel, bolstered and strengthened his kingdom, according to the word of the LORD concerning Israel.
This is the list of David's mighty men: Jashobeam son of Hachmoni was chief of the officers; he wielded his spear against three hundred men, whom he killed at one time.
Next in command was Eleazar son of Dodo the Ahohite, one of the three mighty men.
He was with David at Pas-dammim when the Philistines gathered there for battle. At a place with a field full of barley, the troops fled from the Philistines.
But Eleazar and David stationed themselves in the middle of the field and defended it. They struck down the Philistines, and the LORD brought about a great victory.

units.label: The exploit of the Three at Bethlehem's well

verses.text: Three of the thirty chief men went down to David, to the rock at the cave of Adullam, while a company of Philistines was encamped in the Valley of Rephaim.
At that time David was in the stronghold, and the garrison of the Philistines was at Bethlehem.
David longed for water and said, "Oh, that someone would get me a drink of water from the well near the gate of Bethlehem!"
So the Three broke through the Philistine camp, drew water from the well at the gate of Bethlehem, and brought it back to David. But he refused to drink it; instead, he poured it out to the LORD,

saying, "Far be it from me, my God, to do this! How can I drink the blood of these men who risked their lives?" Because they had brought it at the risk of their lives, David refused to drink it. Such were the exploits of the three mighty men.
Now Abishai, the brother of Joab, was chief of the Three, and he wielded his spear against three hundred men, killed them, and won a name along with the Three.
He was doubly honored above the Three, and he became their commander, even though he was not included among the Three.
And Benaiah son of Jehoiada was a man of valor from Kabzeel, a man of many exploits. He struck down two champions of Moab, and on a snowy day he went down into a pit and killed a lion.

He also struck down an Egyptian, a huge man five cubits tall. Although the Egyptian had a spear like a weaver's beam in his hand, Benaiah went against him with a club, snatched the spear from his hand, and killed the Egyptian with his own spear.
These were the exploits of Benaiah son of Jehoiada, who won a name along with the three mighty men.
He was most honored among the Thirty, but he did not become one of the Three. And David appointed him over his guard.

units.label: The roster of the mighty men
units.note: named by tribe and town, from Bethlehem to Moab

verses.text: Now these were the mighty men: Asahel the brother of Joab, Elhanan son of Dodo of Bethlehem,
Shammoth the Harorite, Helez the Pelonite,
Ira son of Ikkesh the Tekoite, Abiezer the Anathothite,
Sibbecai the Hushathite, Ilai the Ahohite,
Maharai the Netophathite, Heled son of Baanah the Netophathite,
Ithai son of Ribai from Gibeah of the Benjamites, Benaiah the Pirathonite,
Hurai from the brooks of Gaash, Abiel the Arbathite,
Azmaveth the Baharumite, Eliahba the Shaalbonite,
the sons of Hashem the Gizonite, Jonathan son of Shagee the Hararite,
Ahiam son of Sachar the Hararite, Eliphal son of Ur,
Hepher the Mecherathite, Ahijah the Pelonite,
Hezro the Carmelite, Naarai son of Ezbai,
Joel the brother of Nathan, Mibhar son of Hagri,
Zelek the Ammonite, Naharai the Beerothite, the armor-bearer of Joab son of Zeruiah,
Ira the Ithrite, Gareb the Ithrite,
Uriah the Hittite, Zabad son of Ahlai,
Adina son of Shiza the Reubenite, chief of the Reubenites, and the thirty with him,
Hanan son of Maacah, Joshaphat the Mithnite,
Uzzia the Ashterathite, Shama and Jeiel the sons of Hotham the Aroerite,
Jediael son of Shimri and his brother Joha the Tizite,
Eliel the Mahavite, Jeribai and Joshaviah, the sons of Elnaam, Ithmah the Moabite,
Eliel, Obed, and Jaasiel the Mezobaite.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one; one set apart for office, especially king or priest, by the pouring of oil. All Israel anointed David mashiach according to the word of the LORD through Samuel, verse 3, establishing the pattern of God's own chosen and appointed king, the very title that becomes, in Hebrew, Messiah, and in Greek, Christ.

tsiyon.gloss: Zion, originally the name of the Jebusite fortress David captured, later extended to the whole city of Jerusalem and, above all, to the temple mount as the LORD's chosen dwelling place. From this single conquest, Zion becomes one of Scripture's richest words for the place, and the people, where God dwells with His own.

gibbor.gloss: Mighty, a mighty man, a warrior of valor. This chapter's long roster of David's gibborim shows that his kingdom was strengthened not by David alone but by a whole company of valiant men whose strength, the text insists, ultimately came from the LORD's own hand, verse 14.

basic.context: After Saul's death, the Chronicler moves straight to David's coronation, skipping the years of divided kingship covered in 2 Samuel. All Israel comes to David at Hebron, recalling that the LORD had already promised through Samuel that David would shepherd and rule Israel, and the elders make a covenant with him there before anointing him king. Almost immediately David leads Israel to capture Jerusalem from the Jebusites, renaming the captured fortress the City of David, the future home of the ark and the temple. The chapter then honors the mighty men who supported David's rise, especially the Three, whose daring raid to bring David water from Bethlehem's well David responds to by pouring the water out to the LORD rather than drinking it, and it closes with a long roll call of named warriors from across Israel's tribes.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recounting a specific coronation and conquest, compressed and reordered by the Chronicler's theological priorities. He is not concerned to give a full chronological history of David's rise, which took years and involved conflict with Saul's house; he is concerned to show all Israel gathering, as one, around God's anointed king exactly as God had spoken. Reading it well means noticing both what is included, the unity, the divine word, the mighty men's loyalty, and what is deliberately left out.
basic.originalLanguages: David is anointed {{mashiach|king}}, the very Hebrew word behind Messiah and Christ, according to the LORD's own word. He then captures the fortress of {{tsiyon|Zion}}, a name that will grow across Scripture into one of its richest words for God's dwelling with His people. And his kingdom is upheld by a company of {{gibbor|mighty men}}, whose valor the text insists was ultimately the LORD's own gift, verse 14.
basic.typology: David's anointing according to God's spoken word anticipates Christ, installed as King by the Father's own decree and anointed with the Spirit without measure. David's capture of Zion, the future site of the ark and temple, anticipates the true and greater dwelling of God among His people in Christ, and the heavenly Zion gathered at last around Him.
basic.christ: David is anointed king by the will and word of God, exactly as Samuel had spoken years before; so too Jesus, the Son of David, is declared and installed as King by the Father's own decree, Psalm 2:6-7, Acts 2:36, anointed with the Holy Spirit, Acts 10:38. David's capture of Zion, the place where God's presence will dwell, anticipates Christ, in whom God tabernacled among us, John 1:14, and the heavenly Zion, the city of the living God, to which all who trust Him now come, Hebrews 12:22.
basic.god: God is shown here fulfilling His own spoken word: David becomes king exactly as the LORD had promised through Samuel, and David becomes greater and greater because the LORD of Hosts was with him, verse 9. Even the mighty men's victories are credited to the LORD, who brought about a great victory, verse 14, not merely to human skill or courage. God advances His purposes through the willing unity of His people and the courage of ordinary, named men.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Samuel 5:1-10 gives the parallel account of David's anointing and capture of Jerusalem. Psalm 2 celebrates the LORD's anointed king installed on Zion. Acts 2:29-36 applies David's kingship to its fulfillment in the risen Christ. Hebrews 12:22-24 describes believers as having come to Mount Zion, the city of the living God.
basic.oneStory: All Israel gathers with one heart to anoint David king exactly as God had spoken, and David immediately secures Zion, the city that will soon house the ark and later the temple, God's own dwelling among His people. This pattern, God's anointed king established by God's word and surrounded by devoted followers, runs forward across the whole story of Scripture to its fulfillment in Jesus Christ, the Son of David, installed forever as King, in whom God dwells fully among His gathered people.

context.literary: This chapter opens the long narrative section of 1 Chronicles that will occupy the rest of the book, following nine chapters of genealogy and the brief account of Saul's death. It is structured in three clear movements, David's national anointing, the capture of Jerusalem, and the roster of mighty men, each showing a different facet of how David's kingdom was established: by God's word, by military conquest, and by the loyal strength of named individuals.
context.historical: David's coronation at Hebron and capture of Jerusalem, around 1003 BC by common reckoning, transformed a formerly Jebusite stronghold into the political and, soon, spiritual center of Israel, a strategic hilltop city that had resisted Israelite capture since Joshua's time. The naming of so many mighty men by hometown and tribe reflects genuine military records the Chronicler had access to, paralleling the list in 2 Samuel 23 with some variation.
context.canonical: By omitting the years of civil conflict between David's house and Saul's, recorded in 2 Samuel 2-4, the Chronicler presents David's kingship as immediate and nationally unified, a theological simplification that serves his larger purpose: showing his post-exilic readers a model of the whole nation gathering, with one heart, around God's chosen king, a pattern that anticipates the gathering of all peoples around David's greater Son.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show David established as king by the LORD's own spoken word and the willing unity of all Israel, immediately followed by the capture of Jerusalem and the loyal support of his mighty men, so that David's kingship would be understood from its very first page as God's gracious provision rather than mere political achievement.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates specific historical events, a coronation, a conquest, and a roster of warriors; it does not prescribe a pattern for how leaders must be chosen today. What it does teach for every reader is the abiding value of unity around God's appointed purposes, the wisdom of directing even hard-won gifts back to God rather than consuming them for oneself, as David does with the water, and the honor due to those who serve faithfully and courageously alongside a godly leader.

debates.question: Why does the Chronicler skip entirely David's earlier years reigning over Judah alone and the civil war with Saul's house, recorded in 2 Samuel 2 to 4?

views.label: To present David's kingship as immediate, united, and uncontested
views.case: The Chronicler's theological purpose is to show all Israel united behind God's anointed from the start, so omitting years of civil war and rival claims serves his portrait of a king established by national consensus and divine word rather than by protracted conflict.

views.label: Because his post-exilic audience needed a model of unity, not division
views.case: Writing to a fragile, small post-exilic community, the Chronicler emphasizes the ideal of all Israel gathering with one heart around God's chosen king, offering his readers a vision of the unity they themselves needed to recover, rather than dwelling on the historical messiness of David's actual rise.

debates.bearing: Both views agree that the Chronicler is not hiding the history so much as focusing his account on its theological heart: God's king, established by God's own word, gathering God's people around him in unity.

debates.question: Are the exploits of the Three, verses 11-19, meant primarily to honor these individual warriors, or to illustrate something about David and his kingdom?

views.label: Primarily to honor named individuals for their loyalty and courage
views.case: The text names Jashobeam, Eleazar, and the unnamed Three specifically, celebrating individual feats of valor in loving, particular detail, much as a roll of honor would.

views.label: Primarily to illustrate the character David's kingdom cultivated
views.case: David's own response to the water they risked their lives to bring him, pouring it out to the LORD rather than drinking it, becomes the true climax of the story, showing a king who refused to profit from his men's sacrifice and instead offered it back to God, modeling reverent, others-honoring leadership.

debates.bearing: Both are true together: the mighty men's loyalty is genuinely honored by name, and David's own response to their sacrifice reveals the kind of king worth such loyalty, one who directs even his men's devotion back to the LORD.

study.originalLanguages: David becomes {{mashiach|anointed}} king by the united action of all Israel, according to the LORD's own spoken word, the very Hebrew title that becomes Messiah in Hebrew usage and Christ in Greek. He then takes the fortress of {{tsiyon|Zion}}, a single conquered stronghold whose name will expand across the rest of Scripture into one of its richest pictures of God dwelling among His people. And the whole chapter insists that David's kingdom was upheld not by one man alone but by a company of {{gibbor|mighty men}}, whose courage the text repeatedly credits back to the LORD Himself, verse 14.

typology.type: David anointed king by the united action of all Israel, according to the LORD's word
typology.fulfillment: Christ, installed as King by the Father's own decree and welcomed at last by every nation, Psalm 2:6-8, Philippians 2:9-11.

typology.type: David's capture of Zion, the future dwelling place of God among His people
typology.fulfillment: Christ, in whom God tabernacles fully among us, John 1:14, and the heavenly Zion where His people are gathered forever, Hebrews 12:22-24.

typology.type: David refusing to drink water bought at the risk of his men's blood, pouring it out to the LORD
typology.fulfillment: A shadow of the costliness of blood poured out for another, pointing toward Christ, who alone is worthy to receive such devotion, and who Himself poured out His own blood for others rather than requiring theirs, Matthew 26:28.

study.christ: David becomes king exactly as God had spoken through His prophet, anointed, mashiach, by the willing action of all Israel; so too Jesus, the Son of David, is declared King by the Father's own decree, Psalm 2:6-7 fulfilled in Acts 2:36, and anointed with the Holy Spirit without measure, Acts 10:38. David's capture of Zion, the place that will house the ark and, later, the temple, anticipates Christ, in whom the fullness of God dwells bodily, Colossians 2:9, and the heavenly Zion, the city of the living God, to which all who trust in Him now come, Hebrews 12:22-24. Even David's refusal to drink water purchased at the risk of his men's lives, pouring it out to the LORD instead, whispers forward to the one whose own blood, poured out, is alone sufficient and worthy to be received.
study.god: God is shown here fulfilling His own spoken word precisely: David becomes king exactly as the LORD had promised through Samuel years before, verse 3, and David becomes greater and greater because the LORD of Hosts was with him, verse 9. Even the mighty men's victories are explicitly credited to the LORD, who brought about a great victory, verse 14, rather than to human skill or courage alone. God advances His redemptive purposes through the willing unity of His gathered people and the courage of ordinary, named men, showing that no true victory in this chapter is finally anyone's but His.

crossReferences.note: Gives the parallel account of David's anointing at Hebron and his capture of Jerusalem.
Celebrates the LORD's anointed king installed on Zion, a psalm the New Testament applies directly to Christ.
Peter applies David's kingship and God's oath concerning his throne to its fulfillment in the risen and exalted Christ.
Describes believers as having come to Mount Zion, the city of the living God, the ultimate fulfillment of David's conquered stronghold.

study.oneStory: All Israel gathers with one heart to anoint David king exactly as God had spoken through Samuel, and David immediately secures Zion, the city soon to house the ark and, later, the temple, God's own dwelling among His people. This pattern, God's anointed king established by God's own word and surrounded by a company of devoted, valiant followers, runs forward across the whole of Scripture to its fulfillment in Jesus Christ, the Son of David, installed forever as King, in whom God dwells fully among His gathered people from every nation.

quiz.q: On what basis did all Israel come to anoint David king at Hebron?

options.text: According to the word of the LORD spoken through Samuel years before, that David would shepherd and rule Israel.
options.note: Verses 2-3 explicitly ground David's anointing in God's prior spoken promise through Samuel.

options.text: Because David had won a series of decisive military victories over Saul's forces.
options.note: David's earlier military leadership is noted, verse 2, but the explicit basis given is the LORD's own word, not conquest over Saul's house.

options.text: Because the elders simply preferred David's personality to Saul's.
options.note: Understates and misplaces the reason; the text roots the decision in divine promise, not personal preference.

options.text: The text gives no reason for why Israel anointed David.
options.note: False; verses 2-3 clearly state the reason as God's own prior word through Samuel.

quiz.q: What did David do immediately after being anointed king?

options.text: He led Israel to capture Jerusalem, the Jebusite stronghold, making it the City of David.
options.note: Verses 4-7 record the capture of Jerusalem as David's first major act as king.

options.text: He built the temple to house the ark of the covenant.
options.note: Not yet; the temple is built later by Solomon. David does prepare for it, but this chapter records the capture of the city, not the temple's construction.

options.text: He divided the kingdom among his sons.
options.note: Contrary to the text and to the larger biblical record; David unites, rather than divides, the kingdom at this point.

options.text: He immediately went to war against Egypt.
options.note: False; the text describes the capture of Jerusalem from the Jebusites, not a campaign against Egypt.

quiz.q: Why did David refuse to drink the water the Three risked their lives to bring him from Bethlehem's well?

options.text: He considered it equivalent to drinking the blood of the men who had risked their lives for it, so he poured it out to the LORD instead.
options.note: Verse 19 records David's own explanation and his act of pouring the water out to the LORD.

options.text: He was simply not thirsty by the time it arrived.
options.note: Contrary to the text; verse 17 shows David's longing for the water was the reason the Three acted, and his refusal is a deliberate moral response, not indifference.

options.text: He was angry that the men had disobeyed his orders by going.
options.note: Not supported by the text; David is not shown as angry, but as deeply moved by the cost of what they brought him.

options.text: The text does not explain his reasoning.
options.note: False; verse 19 gives his reasoning explicitly and movingly.

quiz.q: What does the text say enabled David's mighty men's victories, as in the stand at Pas-dammim?

options.text: The LORD brought about a great victory, crediting the outcome ultimately to God rather than human skill alone.
options.note: Verse 14 explicitly attributes the victory to the LORD.

options.text: Their superior weapons and training alone secured the victory.
options.note: Their courage and skill are real and celebrated, but the text explicitly credits the ultimate victory to the LORD, not merely to their equipment or training.

options.text: Sheer numerical superiority over the Philistines.
options.note: Contrary to the account, which shows the troops fleeing while Eleazar and David alone held the field, hardly a picture of numerical advantage.

options.text: The text gives no explanation for the victory.
options.note: False; verse 14 explicitly names the LORD as the one who brought about the victory.

quiz.q: How does David's anointing and capture of Zion point forward to Christ?

options.text: As David is anointed king by God's word and takes Zion as God's future dwelling, so Jesus is installed as King by the Father's decree and is Himself the place where God dwells among His people.
options.note: David's anointing and conquest of Zion anticipate Christ's kingship and the true and heavenly Zion, Hebrews 12:22-24.

options.text: It shows only that ancient warfare and kingship are interesting history with no bearing on Christ.
options.note: Understates the text's theological weight; Scripture consistently reads David's kingship and Zion typologically, pointing to Christ.

options.text: It proves that physical cities are more significant to God than the people within them.
options.note: Misplaces the emphasis; Zion matters because it becomes the place of God's dwelling with His people, not for its own sake apart from them.

options.text: It has no connection whatsoever to the New Testament.
options.note: False; both Psalm 2 and Hebrews 12 draw direct lines from David's anointing and Zion to Christ.

questions: All Israel gathered with one heart to make David king exactly as God had promised. Where is God calling you toward unity with others around His purposes, rather than division?

David refused to drink water purchased at the risk of his men's lives, pouring it out to the LORD instead. What costly gift or sacrifice in your own life could you offer back to God rather than keep for yourself?
The mighty men are named individually, honored for specific acts of courage. Whose quiet, faithful courage in your own life deserves more recognition and gratitude from you?
David's kingdom advanced because the LORD of Hosts was with him, not because of human strength alone. Where are you tempted to rely on your own strength rather than trusting God's presence with you?

David's capture of Zion prepared the way for the ark and the temple, the dwelling place of God among His people. How does knowing that God now dwells within you by His Spirit change how you view your own life and body this week?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that even in times past can also be rendered for some time, describing David's earlier military leadership under Saul.
Some Septuagint manuscripts read of the Thirty rather than chief of the officers; compare 2 Samuel 23:8 for the parallel textual tradition.
The Hebrew text here reads the Three where the Syriac reads the Thirty; the BSB follows the Syriac reading, consistent with Abishai's later described status among the Thirty rather than the Three.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 11:1-47. No em dashes or en dashes were present in this passage's source text; none remain in this file.
